narrative:
Let's take a moment to understand the prediction made by the model regarding your bar exam outcome. Currently, the model predicts that you might not pass the bar exam on your first attempt, which led to the rejection of your university application. This prediction is based on several factors from your academic and personal background. Your grades in Year 1 and Year 3, represented by decile scores, are significantly lower than the average, which suggests that your academic performance in those years was below what is typically seen in students who pass the exam. Additionally, your undergraduate GPA is slightly below average, which might have contributed to the model's prediction.

Counterfactuals are essentially "what if" scenarios that help us explore how changes in certain aspects of your profile could lead to a different prediction. They show us the minimum adjustments needed to shift the prediction from failing to passing the bar exam. In your case, the model generated several counterfactual scenarios, each suggesting different combinations of changes that could improve your chances.

One consistent pattern across these scenarios is the need to improve your academic performance, particularly your undergraduate GPA. In some scenarios, a slight increase in your GPA, closer to the average, was enough to change the prediction. Another important factor is your employment status; switching from full-time to part-time work was a common change that helped flip the prediction. This suggests that having more time to focus on your studies could positively impact your exam performance.

Interestingly, some features, like your family income and LSAT score, did not need to change in any scenario, indicating that these aspects are already aligned with those of students who typically pass. The model generated a few counterfactuals, and the most frequent changes involved your GPA and employment status, highlighting these as key areas for improvement.

To increase your chances of passing the bar exam, consider focusing on boosting your GPA through additional coursework or tutoring. Additionally, if feasible, reducing your work hours to part-time could provide you with more time to dedicate to studying. These adjustments, while challenging, are realistic and could significantly enhance your likelihood of success. Remember, these insights are meant to guide you in making informed decisions that align with your goals.
